draft.introduction: Book 7 of the Histories narrates Xerxes’s invasion of Greece and the Battle of Thermopylae (480 BCE), where three hundred Spartans under Leonidas held the pass against the Persian army. The episode became the quintessential story of sacrificial courage.

translation.text: Xerxes asked Demaratus: “Will the Greeks dare to raise a hand against me? Even if all the Greeks and all the men of the western lands were gathered together, they would not be able to withstand my attack.” Demaratus replied: “The Lacedaemonians are the bravest of all. Fighting together they are the best warriors on earth. They are free—yet not entirely free. For law is their master, and they fear it more than your people fear you.”
The epitaph for those who fell at Thermopylae reads: “Stranger, go tell the Lacedaemonians that here we lie, obedient to their words.”
translation.original_text: ὦ ξεῖν', ἀγγέλλειν Λακεδαιμονίοις ὅτι τῇδε κείμεθα τοῖς κείνων ῥήμασι πειθόμενοι

translator_notes.note: Demaratus’s response—that the Spartans obey law (nomos) rather than a human master—articulates the central Greek political idea: freedom under law rather than freedom from all constraint. This concept of ordered liberty profoundly influenced both Roman republicanism and Enlightenment political thought.
